title: Interpreter Injection
text: Interpreter Injection Interpreter injection attacks send malicious code that is intended for execution by one of the interpreters that web applications use, both on the server side and in the browser. Common injection attacks include SQL injection, JavaScript injection, HTTP response splitting, and parameter manipulation. Web applications use interpreters, which may be prone to parameter manipulation attacks, and Web browsers have built-in renderers for HTML, XHTML, XML, and JavaScript. Since web browser may be compromised with spyware or Trojans, they can be abused in phishing attempts by: Faking a website Stealing the user’s cookies and details Trying to resume the user’s session elsewhere Making use of bugs in interpreters to install or run malicious software. Uniface web applications are vulnerable to attack because: Uniface itself is an interpreter, executing ProcScript code on the server. It is possible to invoke public operations from both the client (JavaScript in the browser) and the server (DSP components). If malicious code or data is passed via operation parameters, it can have severe consequences for the application and the database. HTTP headers can contain malicious user input. In HTTP, the header and body are separated by a sequence of two carriage returns and line feeds. A response splitting attack injects two responses, where the first response is the attacker’s payload and the second contains the remainder of the user’s actual output, which is usually ignored by the web server. Defense Uniface is not very vulnerable to SQL injection attacks because it shields you from having to use SQL commands yourself. Although Uniface relies on the JSON to transport data, and JSON uses JavaScript as an interpreter, it has mechanisms in place on both the client and the server to prevent JavaScript injection attacks. On the server, it uses a JSON library to generate correct data. On the client, it uses a native parser (if available) that is immune to this injection problem. If this is not available, a library is used to escape input data. Always validate user data submitted from the web. Ensure that the following ProcScript instructions are not executed with input submitted directly from the web: sql —passes a piece of SQL on to the DBMS path, which could enable an attacker to run queries on the database. proccompile —compiles a piece of ProcScript at runtime. $ude —performs actions on repository data, including compiling, exporting, importing, and converting data, which could enable an attacker to corrupt the Repository or duplicate the application. $expression — returns the result of a non-conditional expression, which could enable an attacker to run arbitrary ProcScript code. $condition —returns the result of a conditional expression, which could enable an attacker to run arbitrary ProcScript. Check for invalidated user input in HTTP headers. You can access header information using the $webinfo("httprequestheaders") ProcScript function. You can access the data using $webinfo("output") . Do not generate an HTML document or error page with unprocessed client input in it. For example: To prevent some <script> insertion using a GET request the < that can be in the $webinfo(“INPUT”) needs to be translated to < before adding it to the output page or raw html field. Otherwise, the browser will execute the script tag during processing the HTML document. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
